title: Saudi FM concludes China visit amid Trump-MBS friction: What to know
gist: Saudi FM Prince Faisal bin Farhan's two-day Beijing visit -- coming as MBS-Trump personal ties sour and Riyadh chafes at US requests during the Iran war -- signals Saudi Arabia is hedging toward China as a security and economic counterweight, leaning on Beijing's leverage over Tehran while deepening trade, crude sales and defense-industrial ties.

themes.theme: Riyadh hedges between Washington and Beijing

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia is visibly hedging its geopolitical alignment between Washington and Beijing, sending Foreign Minister Prince Faisal bin Farhan on a two-day China visit just two weeks after the signing of the US-Iran memorandum of understanding.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: A core Gulf hedging signal: watch for Riyadh diversifying security and economic dependencies away from a US-only posture, a multi-year positioning shift relevant to Gulf-exposed assets.
insights.locator: WASHINGTON -- Two weeks after the signing

insights.claim: The war's security fallout is pushing Saudi Arabia to widen its circle, using Beijing as both a diplomatic counterweight to Washington and a partner capable of engaging Tehran directly.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Reframes China's Gulf role from purely commercial to a genuine diplomatic lever on Iran -- relevant to how any future Gulf-Iran de-escalation gets brokered.

insights.claim: Eurasia Group's Firas Maksad says it is natural for Riyadh to consult Beijing now because China has by far the greatest leverage over Iran and already played the key role brokering the 2023 Saudi-Iran normalization.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Names the specific mechanism -- China's Iran leverage -- that makes Beijing structurally relevant to any Gulf-Iran de-escalation track, not just a trade partner.
insights.quote: China is by far the country with the greatest leverage over Iran, and it had played the key role in mediating and brokering the normalization of Saudi Arabia’s relationship with Iran in 2023

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia is reportedly planning to host reconciliation talks between Gulf countries and Iran, according to Agence France-Presse.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term event to watch -- Saudi-hosted Gulf-Iran talks would be the clearest evidence yet of Riyadh's diplomatic diversification paying off.

insights.claim: Firas Maksad and Ian Bremmer wrote in Foreign Affairs that China is the 'chief geopolitical beneficiary' of the postwar regional shift in the Middle East.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: A blunt framing from established analysts that the net winner of the Iran war's regional fallout, geopolitically, is Beijing rather than Washington.
insights.locator: In a recent piece for Foreign Affairs

insights.claim: China has become Saudi Arabia's largest trading partner over the past decade, with bilateral trade surpassing $107 billion in 2024 according to China's Foreign Ministry.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Quantifies the economic gravity underlying the diplomatic tilt -- $107B+ in trade gives China real leverage over Riyadh's calculus independent of politics.
insights.locator: China has become Saudi Arabia’s largest trading partner

insights.claim: China purchased $47.91 billion of Saudi crude in 2024, and in 2025 bought an average 1.4 million barrels per day from Saudi Arabia, accounting for about 14% of China's total crude oil imports that year.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Saudi Arabia's status as a top-tier crude supplier to China is a structural anchor for the relationship regardless of near-term political friction with Washington -- relevant to oil-flow and pricing-power analysis.

insights.claim: In mid-June 2026 Saudi Arabia signed six agreements and memorandums of understanding with Chinese entities on development and housing projects worth more than $506 million in Riyadh and Dammam.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Shows the economic relationship is broadening beyond energy into real estate and development -- a diversification signal for China-Saudi capital flows.
insights.locator: In mid-June, Saudi Arabia signed six agreements

themes.theme: US-Saudi friction since the Iran war

insights.claim: During the Iran war, Riyadh reportedly denied Washington use of certain bases and airspace for a proposed escort operation through the Strait of Hormuz, according to a May New York Times report.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence Saudi military cooperation with the US has real limits even during an active regional war -- a data point for assessing US basing/access reliability in the Gulf.
insights.locator: In May, The New York Times reported

insights.claim: The personal relationship between Mohammed bin Salman and Donald Trump has visibly soured: their last reported phone call was May 30, and in March Trump publicly said MBS 'didn't think he'd be kissing my ass' after once viewing the US as weak.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: Personal-relationship deterioration at the top is a leading indicator that can precede or accelerate policy-level divergence between the two governments.
insights.quote: didn’t think he’d be kissing my ass

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia has rebuffed US pressure to normalize relations with Israel and grown more critical of the current Israeli government, while its pledged $1 billion to Trump's 'Board of Peace' had reportedly not been paid as of late May, and it has withdrawn LIV Golf funding after the 2026 season and backed out of a funding deal with the Metropolitan Opera.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: A cluster of concrete pullbacks -- unpaid pledge, LIV Golf and Met Opera funding withdrawals -- signals Saudi soft-power and financial disengagement from US-aligned initiatives, a tangible marker of the rift beyond rhetoric.
insights.locator: Saudi Arabia has also rebuffed US pressure

insights.claim: Reports from March indicate Saudi Arabia and China struck a $5 billion deal to build a production line for Wing Loong-3 combat drones in Jeddah, a joint facility between the Aviation Industry Corporation of China and Saudi Arabia's General Authority for Military Industries manufacturing about 48 drones a year; neither government has confirmed the deal.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: An unconfirmed but reported $5B drone manufacturing deal would mark a material escalation of China-Saudi defense-industrial ties, a watch item for Gulf arms-relationship diversification away from US suppliers.

insights.claim: Al-Monitor's analysis concludes the Iran war dented US credibility across the Middle East, pushing Gulf states to build out independent security architecture and diversify partners without necessarily abandoning the US relationship -- the lesson being that dependence on a single power is not a sound option when regional security is in flux.
insights.speaker: Joyce Karam
insights.so_what: The strategic takeaway for the region: expect continued multi-alignment (US plus China plus others) from Gulf states rather than a clean break from Washington -- important for calibrating how much weight to put on any single US-Gulf friction headline.
insights.quote: When regional security is in flux, dependence on a single power is not a sound option.
